data.title: Venezuela’s furious streets forces are angry, armed and asking questions
data.description:
When explosions boomed in the night and US warplanes roared in the sky over Caracas, Jorge Suarez and his companions rushed fearfully for their guns.
For these members of the colectivos – armed loyalists of the leftist leadership – the US raid that ousted Nicolas Maduro as their president was the most dramatic challenge yet.
“We’re not used to it – it was like a bestseller, like something out of a film,” said Suarez, in black sunglasses and a cap bearing the slogan: “Doubt is treason.”
“We took to the streets, waiting for instructions from our leaders.”
As proud defenders of the Venezuelan leadership’s socialist “Bolivarian revolution”, the ousting of Maduro has left them furious and bewildered, convinced that he was betrayed by close allies.

“There is frustration, anger and a will to fight,” said a 43-year-old member of one collective, the Boina Roja – which translates to Red Beret – who identified himself only as Willians, in a black cap and hooded jacket.
“It’s still not really clear what happened … What is clear is that there were many betrayals,” he added, pointing to implausible failures in Maduro’s defences.
“We don’t understand how the anti-aircraft system failed. We don’t know what happened with the rocket-launch system.”
Established in their current form under Maduro’s predecessor Hugo Chavez, the colectivos are tasked with keeping social order on the streets – but are accused by opponents of beating and intimidating rivals.
They have closed ranks behind Delcy Rodriguez, Maduro’s former deputy who took over as interim president.
She has pledged to cooperate with US President Donald Trump over his demand for access to Venezuela’s huge oil reserves – but has insisted the country is not “subordinate” to Washington.
Willians said the colectivos were resisting certain post-Maduro narratives, which he dismissed as mind games – such as “that Trump might bomb again, or that Delcy Rodriguez is with the United States”.
They respect her ideological pedigree – Rodriguez is the daughter of a far-left militant who died in the custody of the intelligence services in 1976.

“I don’t think anyone would betray her father,” said Alfredo Canchica, leader of another collective, the Fundacion 3 Raices. “You can betray the people, but not your father.”
Colectivo members declined to be drawn on how the post-Maduro phase might play out under Trump and Rodriguez, however.
“We don’t believe the threats that the Americans are going to come, dig in and take us out,” Canchica said. “They’ll have to kill us first.”
Feared by opponents as a rifle-wielding, motorbike-mounted shock force, the colectivos are welcomed in some neighbourhoods where they are credited with preventing crime – and where authorities hand out subsidised food parcels.
Speaking at the Chato Candela baseball stadium in the working-class 23 de Enero district, Canchica rejected the negative image they have gained.
When opposition demonstrators and some world powers were accusing Maduro of stealing an election in July 2014, “we stopped the shantytowns from rising up”, he said.
The colectivos also claim to run sports programmes, coordinate with hospitals and transport networks, and visit traders to keep price speculation in check.

Fiercely committed to the “Chavista” cause, they felt the sting of betrayal in Maduro’s capture.
“The betrayal must have come from someone very close to our commander [Maduro]”, Canchica said.
“It was so perfect we didn’t notice, and we still don’t know who betrayed us, how they betrayed us – it happened so fast.”
In his office with images of independence hero Simon Bolivar, Chavez and Maduro on the walls, and books, bullets and a sound-wave bomb on the table, Suarez bitterly recalled watching animated reconstructions of Maduro’s capture published online.
“It makes you angry,” he said. “Despite all the support Commander [Vladimir] Putin, China and North Korea have given us militarily, how can we react in real time when [the US] has more advanced technology than we do?”

data.title: Shareholder capitalism is taking Asian family businesses in a new direction
data.description:
There were plenty of fireworks in major stock markets during 2025. Wall Street plunged after US President Donald Trump’s “Liberation Day” tariff barrage, only to end the year near a record high while Tokyo’s Nikkei index surged through the 50,000 mark. It is little surprise that significant developments in private equity markets in and beyond Asia went largely unnoticed.
Shareholder capitalism continues to evolve outside the headlines. The pace of change is accelerating in Asia especially, not least in Japan. The latest stage of evolution involves an increasing shift from public to private equity financing, apparently reversing the decades-long trend of companies seeking a wider shareholder base via stock exchange listing of their shares.
This has particular significance in a region where family-owned companies have long dominated the economic landscape. The shift implies a dilution of family ownership in favour of greater external ownership and control, albeit concentrated more in the hands of financial institutions and less in those of individual investors.
Family businesses form the backbone of the Asian economy, with most companies in the Asia-Pacific owned by a family group. According to global leadership advisory firm Russell Reynolds, of the top 750 global family businesses ranked by revenue in 2020, over 20 per cent were Asia-based, with total revenue of almost US$2 trillion.
Changes taking place in equity markets mark a tacit recognition that a continuing reduction in the number of companies listed on global stock markets is occurring at a time of rising inflows of savings into investment institutions, resulting in more frequent and bigger stock bubbles. This drives the search for new equity and other assets via private markets.
There is still a huge amount of potentially investible assets untapped by stock market investors because they are held by unlisted companies. Where family ownership remains high, as in Asia, companies have been reluctant to go public because of the onerous financial disclosure and other regulatory requirements that a stock exchange listing entails.

This is changing as increasing numbers of private companies are taking the private equity route to obtain new finance by widening their investor base through participation by private equity funds. In many cases these assets are offloaded eventually by private funds via stock markets, but the overall funding process is becoming more indirect and less transparent.
Nikkei Asia noted in a report published on December 26 that “an accommodative interest rate environment and the global stock market boom could pave the way for a stronger 2026” as “more investor capital is expected to flow into private equity”. Citing data from asset servicing firm Ocorian, the report said that the global value of private assets held in funds jumped by around 10 per cent in 2025 to an all-time high of more than US$14 trillion. It suggested the value of private funds would reach nearly US$24 trillion within five years, with private equity almost doubling to US$17.4 trillion.
This is still dwarfed by the US$127 trillion total capitalisation of global stock markets, but public equity markets are increasingly seen now as being in, or approaching, a boom or bubble phase, especially in the tech and artificial intelligence sectors. They could therefore be subject to a correction, whereas private markets are in a more stable phase of their evolution.

Private equity’s focus on the Asia-Pacific has grown by leaps and bounds in the past few years. Private equity funds targeting the Asia-Pacific raised close to US$800 billion between 2015 and 2019 and around 28 per cent of all private equity assets under management in 2021 were focused on the region, according to Russell Reynolds.
Historically, many family-owned businesses in Asia and elsewhere have not been inclined to accept funding from private equity investors. They typically view such investors as being more interested in making a quick profit than improving the company’s long-term performance. More recently, however, various factors have nudged family businesses to explore funding through the sale of a minority or even majority stakes.
One is a lack of successors in Asian family-owned firms, the Russell Reynolds study noted. “The next generation is not always able or willing to step into the shoes of their elders. Bringing in the right private equity partner allows family firms to ensure business continuity and institutionalisation to facilitate sustained growth.”

Family firms that dominate the economic landscape are now looking for financial partners to invest in new asset classes or new economy businesses that are complementary or add to their existing operations. Private equity involvement is also seen as an attractive option for growth if family businesses wish to avoid the complexities of going public, which include managing more stakeholders and a reduced ability to be agile in the business environment.
In Japan, a catalyst has been reforms in the Tokyo Stock Exchange, with the bourse pushing listed companies to improve their capital efficiency and more businesses looking to offload their noncore assets. “Undervalued companies also face pressure to go private. So, there are a lot of tailwinds for buyout investments in Japan,” the Nikkei Asia report observed.
These changes to the anatomy of corporate Asia and beyond point to an increasing institutional presence in ownership and control of business and less emphasis on what former British prime minister Margaret Thatcher used to refer to as the need for greater “shareholder democracy”. They could also be a prelude to greater coordination of business and wider economic objectives, which is to be welcomed.

data.title: China actor rejects US$2,800 fee to be mountain-climbing companion, plans to change jobs
data.description:
A Chinese actor who once went viral for offering paid companionship services to people evening hiking on Mount Tai for 799 yuan (US$115) has revealed that the charge was inflated to 19,999 yuan (US$2,800).
He now plans to launch a paid swimming companionship service in 2026.
In June, 35-year-old Shi Yuanting, a native of Shenyang in northeastern China’s Liaoning province, went viral after posting a video of himself accompanying a client on a hike up Mount Tai.

He charged 699 yuan during the day and 799 yuan at night.
In the video, Shi is seen gasping for air while joking about being an actor without gigs.
He confidently shows off his abs, claiming he fully meets the physical standards of a professional Mount Tai climbing companion.
Shi graduated from the Central Academy of Drama, one of China’s most prestigious acting schools.

In 2022, he gained recognition for playing the leading role of Wang Hu in the comedy The Newcomer, which was well received, earning over 39,000 ratings and a 7.9 score on a popular Chinese social networking and review platform.
Despite appearing in many productions, acting roles have become scarce for Shi in recent years.
“I love sport and I try to stay positive in life. When I do not have acting work, I take part-time jobs to support myself and experience life more deeply. This might give me more insight than other actors when I get a chance to perform again,” he said.

After trending online, Shi received offers to star in several short dramas. His daily rate rose from 2,000 to 3,000 yuan (US$290 to US$430) to around 5,000 yuan.
“I received some short drama offers in the second half of the year. I am happy just getting short drama roles,” he told Jiupai News.
He has now accompanied over a dozen clients and insists on providing a “non-sexual, purely green” service, charging a flat fee of 799 yuan for evening companionship.
“At its peak, my social media inbox was flooded with messages. Some even offered up to 19,999 yuan. I felt that was too much and did not dare to accept. I just told the I was purely green,” said Shi.
“Then the person seriously asked me: ‘Purely green? Then why would I pay so much for you?’”
“I do not want to ruin a positive and healthy initiative with any inappropriate behaviour. I would rather earn less than make money that way.”
After going viral, a scenic spot also invited Shi to perform, typically starting at 10am and finishing around 7 or 8pm.
Each day, he performs multiple short acts, usually 10 to 20 minutes per session, but must maintain high energy throughout.

Shi said that if he does not land any acting roles next year, he plans to become a “swimming companion”, leveraging his qualifications as a nationally certified swimmer.
“A middle-class family saw my swimming videos and asked if I would be willing to accompany their child. I have also received online inquiries, priced at about HK$1,500 (US$190) an hour. I think it is worth a try. I can offer professional guidance and ensure their safety.”
Shi has received widespread praise on Chinese social media.
One person said: “Graduated from a top drama school and still no roles? Being a hiking companion is great. At least it builds physical fitness.”
While another added: “Honestly, this is a good thing. Survival is what matters most.”

data.title: Horse power: Chinese brands look to boost sales with Lunar New Year items
data.description:
As the Chinese zodiac’s Year of the Horse approaches, distiller Kweichow Moutai has released a limited promotional line of its baijiu sorghum liquor commemorating the occasion as it seeks to bounce back from a prolonged sales slump – a marketing strategy being adopted by several brands in China as they look to give their revenues a festive boost.
On Tuesday, Moutai launched three limited Year of the Horse editions priced between 1,899 yuan and 3,789 yuan (US$272 to US$542) a bottle.
The company said all varieties sold out shortly after going on sale. Speculators have since been offering to buy the entry-level bottle for around 3,000 yuan, while on social media platforms such as RedNote, resale prices for the most expensive offering have climbed to around 8,000 yuan.
Moutai rolled out its first zodiac-themed product in 2014, also the Year of the Horse. Bottles were priced at 849 yuan, with production limited to 350 tonnes (385.8 short tons), and later traded for around 20,000 yuan on the secondary market.
However, last year’s Year of the Snake bottles, priced from 2,499 yuan, saw far more muted demand, with resale prices only modestly higher.
The horse is the seventh animal in the 12-year cycle of the Chinese zodiac and is considered a symbol of vitality, energy, perseverance and success – qualities Moutai may be looking to emulate after a drop in sales.
Revenue and net profit growth slowed to about 6 per cent year on year in the first three quarters of 2025, the weakest pace in nearly a decade, with third-quarter growth hovering around 0.5 per cent.
Prices for its flagship Flying Fairy line, which peaked at about 3,400 yuan a bottle in 2021, have fallen below 1,500 yuan amid a property downturn, subdued banquet spending and shifting consumer preferences.
Formerly a ubiquitous status symbol at weddings, business dinners and state functions, liquor from the state-backed giant became an investment-grade collectible during the boom years. At its peak, the company recorded a nearly US$506 billion market capitalisation in early 2021.
Moutai is not alone in trying to reap the benefits of some festive cheer, with at least 11 domestic liquor brands – along with global names such as Hennessy, Johnnie Walker and Don Julio 1942 – having rolled out Year of the Horse releases ahead of celebrations for the next Lunar New Year.
And zodiac-driven offerings are not limited to liquor brands, with commemorative gold coins being issued by China National Gold Group Corporation and the People’s Bank of China. International mints including PAMP Suisse, Australia’s Perth Mint and Poland’s Germania Mint have also released Year of the Horse pieces.
Hong Kong jeweller Chow Tai Fook also attracted attention with a horse and cow themed pendant meant to symbolise diligence and good fortune. However, this product sparked mockery online after Chinese internet users took it to represent “cattle and horses”, a popular slang term for overworked employees.

data.title: From memes to the streets: Gen Z’s fight back against corruption
data.description:
In 2025, youth-led protests erupted everywhere from Morocco to Nepal, Madagascar and Europe. A generation refused to remain silent in the face of economic precariousness, corruption and eroding democratic norms and institutions.
Although they arose in different contexts, all the protests were met with the same playbook of responses: repression, contempt and suspicion towards youth dismissed as irresponsible.
In Morocco, the #Gen212 movement, which originated on social media, denounced the high cost of living, police violence, muzzling of civil society and lack of opportunities. This mobilisation, which began digitally on platforms such as Discord, quickly spilled over from screens into concrete action taken in several cities across the country.
In Madagascar, young people took to the streets at the end of September in a climate of high pre-election tensions to demand real change before being violently repressed. In Nepal, thousands of young people occupied public spaces, demanding genuine democracy and an end to the corruption that is undermining the country.

In Europe, too, youth are mobilising against authoritarian excesses and persistent inequalities. In Italy, France and Spain, young people are taking to the streets to protest gender-based violence, unpopular reforms and police repression and to demand recognition of their political rights.
Although the contexts are very different, these movements share the same goal of refusing injustice and demanding that marginalised voices be heard.
These movements are often treated as fleeting emotional outbursts, even though they express structured political demands for social justice, freedom, economic security, access to dignity and participation.
Yet the responses by governments have been heading in a totally different direction – towards increased repression. Young protesters are being monitored, arrested, stigmatised and sometimes accused of treason or of being manipulated by foreign powers.
In Morocco, for example, nearly 2,500 young people have been prosecuted, with more than 400 convicted – including 76 minors – since September. The charges include “group rebellion”, “incitement to commit crimes” and taking part in armed gatherings. More than 60 prison sentences have been handed down, some of them for up to 15 years.
This mass judicialisation of a peaceful movement has been denounced by Amnesty International, which points to excessive uses of force and the increasing criminalisation of protest.

In Madagascar, the response was just as brutal: at least 22 deaths, more than 100 injuries and hundreds of arbitrary arrests were recorded during youth demonstrations against corruption and electoral irregularities.
According to the United Nations, security forces used rubber bullets and tear gas to disperse the crowds. The crisis culminated in the flight of President Andry Rajoelina, which confirmed that, far from defusing the conflict, the crackdown revealed institutions’ fragility in the face of politicised youth.
The actions of the young people who have been arrested during recent protests are often attributed to a lack of parental responsibility.
In Morocco, for example, the Home Office has called on parents to supervise and guide their children. In Indonesia, the Philippines, Peru and Nepal alike, authorities call on parents to supervise, guide or restrain their children, shifting the political conflict into the family sphere.
This trend illustrates what national security researcher Fatima Ahdash calls the “familialisation” of politics: instead of addressing the social, economic and ideological causes of protests, governments turn them into a matter of home education, depoliticising, individualising and privatising the protests in the process. Families become the prism through which young people’s political behaviour is interpreted, evaluated and sometimes punished.

This response is not new, but it is taking on unprecedented proportions in a global context of democratic fragility and authoritarian recentring of power marked by the restriction of freedoms, the control of protest and the criminalisation of social movements.
States are adopting a defensive stance, treating youth engagement not as a civic resource but as a threat to be neutralised. This hardened stance is symptomatic of a deeper problem: youth are refusing to be satisfied with empty promises and forced compromises, but they face powers unable to recognise the legitimacy of their anger and aspirations.
Repression in response to criticism has become a tactic governments use to avoid being questioned. But this strategy is becoming increasingly fragile.
That’s because first, it denies the legitimacy of the anger being expressed. Second, it ignores a fundamental reality: that this anger is rooted in collective experiences of social decline, discrimination and political powerlessness. It’s not empty anger. It expresses a demand for social, political and environmental change that institutions are struggling to grasp.
Unlike mobilisation like the Arab spring of 2011, the current protests led by Generation Z are horizontal; they are decentralised, have no identifiable leaders and are rooted in the urgency of the present.
They also originate on social media, organise themselves into autonomous micro-cells, reject structuring ideological narratives, and favour a politics of everyday life – meaning they reject precariousness while calling for immediate dignity and concrete justice.
Their aesthetic is fluid, borrowing from digital codes – memes, manga, visual remixes – and their forms circulate through emotional affinities rather than imitation. This makes them elusive to the powers that be, but powerfully viral.

These movements stir up political emotions (anger, but also hope) and create new languages, digital practices and forms of engagement that often lie outside traditional parties.
One unifying visual element keeps coming up: the black flag with a skull and crossbones wearing a straw hat, a symbol taken from the manga One Piece. More than just a nod to pop culture, this Jolly Roger embodies a thirst for justice, freedom and rebellion shared by a globalised youth, from Kathmandu to Rome.
In Serbia, for example, a student uprising in early 2025 with no visible leader united thousands of people around a simple slogan: more democracy. The movement spread to other generations, without any party or hierarchy, challenging a government that tried to stifle the protests through force and stigmatisation.
Meanwhile, the young people of Cuba Decide are mobilising on digital platforms to demand a democratic referendum in the midst of constant surveillance. Thanks to encrypted tools and alliances abroad, they are circumventing censorship and amplifying their voices beyond borders.
While criminalising young people and their protests may slow their momentum, it doesn’t solve anything. It only undermines the social contract, fuels political disenchantment and reinforces polarisation. What’s more, it risks pushing demands for reform to outright refusal of the status quo.
Recent protests remind us of an obvious fact: young people are not “the future”, but political entities in the present. Governments need to hear not just the noise of protest, but the clarity of the demands: justice, dignity, representation and a future.
Amani Braa is an assistant lecturer of sociology at the University of Montreal. This article was first published by The Conversation.

data.title: Hong Kong’s finance chief confirms early surplus, vows more elderly support
data.description:
Hong Kong’s finance chief has confirmed an early operating account surplus driven by strong financial market performance and stock trading stamp duty revenue, as he addressed calls for more elderly welfare measures at the first public forum for the coming budget.
Financial Secretary Paul Chan Mo-po vowed on Saturday to make “utmost” efforts to address elderly residents’ concerns over high living costs, while highlighting that authorities had not reduced public spending on the social welfare sector in the past financial year despite the budget deficit.
Chan was speaking to about 100 residents who joined a public forum to express their views ahead of the 2026-27 government budget, scheduled to be unveiled in late February.
“Despite the trade war and rising geopolitics last year, Hong Kong still recorded 3.2 per cent economic growth, with the economy now showing steady and orderly development,” he told the forum.
“Thanks to the financial market performance, we gained more [stock market] stamp duty, which enabled us to achieve [operating account] surplus earlier.”
In last year’s budget, Chan forecast the government’s operating account would return to a surplus only in 2026-27 after recording a deficit for three consecutive years.
But the finance minister conceded that the government’s capital account, under which public money was used to speed up the Northern Metropolis megaproject and other infrastructure building, would still face pressure.
He also noted an uneven pace of industry development in different sectors but acknowledged that economic transformation required time.
“Despite that, the authorities did not reduce spending on livelihood areas such as healthcare, social welfare and education, which account for 59 per cent of recurrent expenditure in the current financial year,” Chan told the forum.
In response to calls to take better care of the ageing population, he said authorities must address the needs precisely, while emphasising the “user-pays” principle and unchanged commitment to ensuring proper care for the general public.
Hong Kong’s stock exchange earlier reported a surge in IPO activity in the first quarter of last year, with 17 new listings attracting a total of HK$18.7 billion (US$2.4 billion), nearly quadrupling year-on-year figures.
The government earlier said it expected stamp duties to generate HK$67 billion in 2025-26, and that the operating account would return to a surplus in 2026-27.
Six of the 25 participants who spoke at the forum asked if the government would allocate more resources to support the city’s elderly.
One of the participants, surnamed Lau, asked whether the government would provide free transport for elderly residents aged 70 and above, similar to many other places around the world.
Starting this April, adjustments will be made to the HK$2 elderly transport concession scheme, with fares above HK$10 being entitled to only an 80 per cent discount.
Another participant, a woman surnamed Fung, asked how the government would allocate more capital to support the elderly despite the deficit, rather than simply pushing them to relocate to other Greater Bay Area cities.
The bay area refers to a new national economic powerhouse that links Hong Kong with Macau and nine Guangdong cities.
“We are well aware of the concerns of seniors regarding the rising cost of living, and we will do our best to support them to the best of our ability,” Chan said.
The government launched public consultations for this year’s budget on December 17, with Chan saying authorities would focus on how the city could boost economic development and better integrate into the nation’s latest five-year plan.
The government recorded a deficit of HK$56.8 billion in the first seven months of the 2025-26 financial year, while its reserves stood at HK$597.5 billion as of October.

data.title: Indonesia blocks Elon Musk’s Grok chatbot over AI porn concerns
data.description:
Indonesia temporarily blocked Elon Musk’s Grok chatbot on Saturday due ‌to the risk of AI-generated pornographic content, becoming ‍the first country to deny access to the AI tool.
The move comes after governments and regulators from Europe to Asia have ⁠condemned the app, with some opening inquiries into sexualised content.
xAI, the start-up behind Grok, said on Thursday it was restricting image generation and editing to paying ‍subscribers as it tried to fix safeguard lapses that had ‍allowed sexualised outputs including depictions of scantily clad children.
“The government views the practice of ‌non-consensual sexual deepfakes as a serious violation of human rights, ‍dignity, and the security of citizens in the digital space,” Communications and Digital Minister Meutya Hafid said in a statement.
The ministry has also summoned ‌X officials ⁠to discuss the matter.
Musk said on X that anyone using Grok to make illegal content would suffer the same consequences as if they had uploaded illegal content.
xAI replied ‌to an email seeking comment with what seemed to be an automated response: “Legacy Media Lies”. ‌X did not immediately ‌respond to a request for comment.
Indonesia, with the world’s biggest Muslim population, has strict rules that ban the sharing online of content deemed obscene.

data.title: K-drama casting news about Park Min-young, Song Joong-ki, Im Si-wan and more
data.description:
Love is in the air – and undercover – in this month’s K-drama casting announcements, which find Korea’s top stars circling some novel twists on K-drama standards.
Read on to find out more.
1. Nine to Six
Park Min-young (Marry My Husband) and Yook Sung-jae of the K-pop outfit BTOB are in talks to lead the coming SBS drama Nine to Six, and representatives have confirmed the discussions. The project is an adaptation of the Chinese series The Rational Life.
Park is eyeing the role of Kang Ji-yi, a lawyer working on the legal team of a car company who has sidelined romance to focus on her career.
Yook is looking over the part of Han Seon-woo, a law school graduate turned jewellery designer. Mature beyond his years, he tends to act from the heart, unlike the cool-headed Ji-yi.

Known for hits ranging from City Hunter to What’s Wrong with Secretary Kim, Park was last on screen in the comedy caper Confidence Queen. Yook’s K-drama appearances include The Golden Spoon and last year’s The Haunted Palace.
Nine to Six is the latest series from director Lee Hyung-min, who is known for popular shows like Strong Girl Bong-soon and Miss Night and Day.
2. Love Cloud
After sharing the screen in Reborn Rich, one of the most successful K-dramas of all time, Song Joong-ki and Park Ji-hyun may reunite for the JTBC series Love Cloud, with agencies for both confirming that they are currently in talks to join the project.

Song has been offered the part of Kang Woo-joo, who used to be a flight instructor and now works as an air traffic controller. Meanwhile, Park may take on the role of Ahn Ha-nee, a daredevil first officer who pilots planes for a commercial airline.
The pair first met seven years ago when Ha-nee was earning her wings under Woo-joo’s instruction. Now, they reunite on Jeju Island.
Star of Descendants of the Sun and Vincenzo, Song was last seen in the romantic drama My Youth, a rare ratings miss for the actor.
Park has been going from strength to strength in recent years, appearing in commercial hits like Flex X Cop and critical ones such as You and Everything Else.

3. My Guilty Human
Im Si-wan (Squid Game season 2) and Seol In-ah (Business Proposal) have been set as the leading stars of the tvN drama My Guilty Human, which is based on the popular web novel Why Don’t You Know She’s a Woman? – itself previously adapted as a webtoon of the same name.
Im is set to play Yoon Yi-jun, the haughty heir of the all-powerful chaebol Taegang Group, who suddenly becomes a murder suspect.
Seol will star as a woman who used to be a special forces officer; she then goes undercover as a male personal secretary to investigate Yi-jun. Aside from her murder investigation, she must also learn to deal with Yi-jun, who is a notoriously prickly boss.

Mixing thrills, romance and workplace comedy, My Guilty Human should offer no shortage of guilty pleasures for K-drama fans.
Following last summer’s Squid Game finale, Im was seen in the Kill Boksoon spin-off Mantis. Seol’s last role was in Oh My Ghost Clients. Im and Seol previously collaborated on the disaster film Emergency Declaration.
4. I’m Against My Romance
Kim Jae-won (Hierarchy) may be stepping in to replace Lee Jong-suk, who passed on an offer to lead the coming series I’m Against My Romance. Before Lee, Choi Hyun-wook (Twinkling Watermelon) had declined the leading male role of a project that was initially announced three years ago.
Kim’s agency confirmed that he is reviewing an offer to appear in the series, which follows the reunion of a man and woman who were each other’s first loves.

Casting for the female lead remains up in the air after the role was turned down by Go Min-si. There has not been an update on who might fill the director’s chair, which was vacated by Lee Eung-bok (Descendants of the Sun, Sweet Home).
The major reshuffle is not necessarily cause for concern, as individual projects tend to be at the mercy of flexible production schedules.
Last year’s The Price of Confession, with Jeon Do-yeon and Kim Go-eun, is a perfect example of a project that was originally set up with a completely different director and stars (Song Hye-kyo and Han So-hee) and still became a buzzy show.

data.title: System failure at Malaysia-Singapore border strands thousands of travellers
data.description:
Tens of thousands of travellers were left stranded at Malaysia’s two main land checkpoints with Singapore on Saturday after a major system failure shut down most automated immigration gates, causing hours-long queues at one of the world’s busiest border crossings.
The worst congestion was reported at the Sultan Iskandar Building (BSI) Customs, Immigration and Quarantine Complex in Johor Bahru, where foreign visitors faced waits of up to two hours to clear immigration.
The problem also affected the nearby Sultan Abu Bakar Complex, though officials said conditions there remained manageable.
At least 39 autogates at BSI’s entry points and 29 at the exit areas of the bus halls had been experiencing intermittent faults for several days before a surge of weekend traffic caused the system to crash completely.

Malaysian citizens using local passports were still able to use functioning autogates, and vehicle and motorcycle clearance systems were not affected. However, the outage also extended to the KTM railway station at BSI, a security official said.
“This is the first major disruption this year, and the timing is bad especially when we are getting a lot of foreign visitors in conjunction with Visit Malaysia and Visit Johor 2026,” the official said, adding that teams were working to restore the system in stages.
The Border Control and Protection Agency has instructed immigration officers to open all manual counters to ease congestion. The agency is preparing an official notice urging travellers to expect delays.
A similar incident in July last year disabled autogates at multiple checkpoints across Malaysia, affecting more than 380,000 foreign visitors.
Since June 2024, nationals from 63 countries, along with accredited diplomats and their families, have been eligible to use Malaysia’s automated immigration gates for faster clearance.
This article was first published by The Star

data.title: As healthcare costs soar in the Philippines, are doctors’ fees to blame?
data.description:
The Philippine government is exploring ways to cap doctors’ professional fees after public complaints about soaring hospital bills, a move that has drawn resistance from physicians who say they are being unfairly blamed for deeper failures in health financing.
The Department of Health (DOH) said it was studying possible standards for doctors’ fees following social media posts from patients detailing unexpectedly high out-of-pocket costs, but doctors’ groups argue that inadequate insurance coverage and delayed reimbursements are the real drivers of financial strain.
In December, health department spokesman Albert Domingo said on a radio programme that the agency was considering reforms that could help set benchmarks for professional fees, after what he described as complaints about “excessive” charges.
Domingo said doctors in the Philippines currently had wide discretion to set their own fees, with no regulatory body overseeing rates.
“In the world of healthcare, [professional fees] are a very sensitive issue as doctors set their fees,” he said. “But in other countries, doctors don’t set their fees, it’s usually the government or the governing medical association.”
If a doctor sets their fees, we have no choice but to follow that rate
Albert Domingo, health department spokesman
According to Domingo, the lack of regulation could lead to fees that some patients consider excessive, and complicates the government’s ability to fully cover hospital bills through PhilHealth, the country’s state-run insurer.
“Until [regulation happens], it is difficult for the government to pay 100 per cent of patients’ bills because if a doctor sets their fees, we have no choice but to follow that rate,” he said.
He suggested allowing the Philippine Medical Association to lead negotiations of fee ranges, as the organisation “has the moral authority” to address the “excessively wide range of [fees]”.
Domingo’s comments come as controversy regarding medical professionals’ high fees has been brewing for months on social media.
In a viral post August last year, Filipino broadcaster Ramon Tulfo criticised Dr Ramon Ribu, a renowned wound care specialist, for charging a patient 85,000 pesos (US$1,435) in professional fees for what Tulfo described as a “minor surgery” on the patient’s leg.
Members of the medical community rushed to Ribu’s defence, saying he was only doing his job and the fee charged was “not excessive – it was discounted”.
However, some social media users have criticised such comments, saying that most medical expenses are still out of reach of many Filipinos, who subsist on the minimum daily wage of 695 pesos (US$11.73).
DOH Secretary Ted Herbosa last month had to address social media concerns regarding “alleged excessive doctors’ professional fees and hospital charges, and detention of patients due to non-payment of hospital bills” allegedly done in certain hospitals in the province of Pampanga.
Herbosa explained that while regulating doctors’ rates did not fall under the health department’s scope, the DOH and PhilHealth could “strategically purchase professional services based on acceptable norms of each specialty society”.

‘Scapegoating’ doctors
Doctors have refuted the DOH’s claims, arguing that fees are not the primary cause behind high medical expenses, and point towards systemic problems in healthcare costs.
“Reimbursements are delayed and insufficient. The safety net breaks. Healthcare financing collapses. And the response is to probe doctors’ professional fees?” Dr Fidel Cascabel II wrote on social media.
Doctors, he said, were typically “the easiest target” when it came to assigning blame on health costs. “This isn’t reform, it’s scapegoating.”
Cascabel called for the “fixing” of PhilHealth, which had not received any government subsidies last year following scrutiny over its reserve funds.
In December, the Supreme Court struck down the transfer of 89.9 billion pesos (US$1.51 billion) of PhilHealth’s funds to the national treasury as unconstitutional and ordered the return of the initial 60 billion pesos it had remitted to the treasury while blocking the transfer of the remaining 29.9 billion pesos.
This month, the government sought to backtrack on the issue by passing its “highest budget ever” for PhilHealth in its 6.793-trillion-peso national budget, but critics argued that such claims were misleading as the budget included the 60 billion pesos of PhilHealth funds.
The Philippine College of Physicians, the country’s internal medicine society, said in a statement in December that while it “fully acknowledges the financial hardship experienced by many Filipino patients and families, we believe that this framing risks diverting attention from the more fundamental and urgent issue: inadequate health financing by the State, particularly through PhilHealth”.
Professional fees represented the compensation for physicians’ expertise, years of training, continuous professional development and the “immense responsibility of managing life-and-death situations”, it added.
The organisation urged the DOH and policymakers to look into meaningful reforms for PhilHealth, arguing that its limited packages, low and outdated rate caps for cases, and delayed and insufficient coverage were behind high out-of-pocket expenses.
“The real issue is whether our health system is adequately financed so that no Filipino is denied care because of cost,” it said.

Patients’ perspective?
Patient advocates have also called for their perspectives to be included in discussions on out-of-pocket medical expenses, noting that patients are typically left unaware of the real costs of healthcare.
“Honestly, we don’t know where the cost is coming from,” Karen Alparce-Villanueva, president of the Philippine Alliance of Patient Organisations, told This Week in Asia.
Medical societies and agencies should “sit down and tell us what the real cost of care is”, she said. “Why do patients have co-pay when PhilHealth has adjusted its rates? Are we still saying that all the case rates are not enough? How much is enough for the doctors and the facility?”
A September 2025 study by the Philippine Institute for Development Studies found that 99.9 per cent of nearly 9,000 case rates, or the fixed rates for specific health conditions, had not been updated since 2013, as hospital charges continued to increase annually by 3.4 per cent between 2014 and 2023.
This has caused the real value of PhilHealth payments to decline by 40 per cent, with 98.8 per cent of hospital cases now exceeding the case rates set by the insurer, forcing hospitals to pass the costs to patients.
The study also found that out-of-pocket payments still comprised about 44 per cent of total health spending even with insurance coverage.
Alparce-Villanueva agreed with the DOH’s view that medical associations should set a benchmark for these fees, and called for patients to be involved in the conversation about fair and equitable pricing.
“We need to put patients on the boards of PhilHealth and DOH so that patient voices will be there. We are the end recipients of what they are doing … without us then their efforts would be futile,” she said.
